content: Cleopatra spoke nine languages fluently: Egyptian, Greek, Latin, Ethiopian, Hebrew, Aramaic, Arabic, Parthian, and the language of the Medes. She was the first Ptolemaic ruler to actually learn Egyptian, despite her dynasty ruling Egypt for nearly 300 years. Her multilingualism allowed her to negotiate directly with foreign rulers without translators.

Cleopatra was the last ruler of the Ptolemaic dynasty, which was founded by Ptolemy I, one of Alexander the Great's generals, in 305 BC. The Ptolemies were Macedonian Greek in origin and ruled Egypt for nearly 300 years. They built the famous Library of Alexandria and established Egypt as a center of learning and culture in the ancient world.

Cleopatra became co-ruler of Egypt at age 18 alongside her younger brother Ptolemy XIII. When he tried to depose her, she raised an army and allied with Julius Caesar to reclaim her throne. She was a shrewd political leader who used diplomacy, intelligence, and strategic alliances to maintain Egypt's independence against the expanding Roman Empire.

The Library of Alexandria, supported by the Ptolemaic rulers including Cleopatra, was the greatest repository of knowledge in the ancient world. It contained an estimated 400,000 to 700,000 scrolls covering mathematics, astronomy, medicine, philosophy, and literature. Scholars from across the Mediterranean world came to study there.

In 48 BC, Cleopatra famously smuggled herself into Caesar's presence rolled in a carpet (or linen sack). She charmed Caesar with her wit and intelligence, forming both a political and personal alliance. Caesar restored her to the Egyptian throne and she bore him a son, Caesarion. Their alliance secured Egypt's independence from Roman annexation.

By Cleopatra's time, Egyptian civilization was already over 3,000 years old — the pyramids of Giza were as ancient to her as she is to us. She presided over one of the wealthiest kingdoms in the world, controlling vast grain reserves that fed much of the Mediterranean region. Alexandria was a cosmopolitan city rivaling Rome itself.

Contrary to popular depictions focusing only on romance, Cleopatra was a serious scholar. She reportedly wrote treatises on weights and measures, cosmetics, and possibly medicine. She funded scientific research and supported the Museum of Alexandria — essentially the world's first university research institution.

After the defeat at the Battle of Actium in 31 BC against Octavian (later Augustus), Cleopatra chose death over capture and humiliation in a Roman triumph. She died on August 12, 30 BC, reportedly from the bite of an asp (Egyptian cobra). With her death, Egypt became a Roman province, ending 3,000 years of pharaonic rule and the Hellenistic period.
